dashboard.label: AWS DFD Visualizer User Guide
dashboard.description: Comprehensive interactive reference and live demonstration of the React & D3.js custom AWS Data Flow Diagram visualizer.

div.style: padding: 15px; background-color: #1e2832; border-radius: 6px; border: 1px solid #2d3e50; margin-bottom: 20px;
div.h1: AWS Security Data Flow Diagram (DFD) Visualizer
div.p: Welcome to the AWS-DFD-Visualizer, a React-based custom visualization for Splunk built to render complex AWS Config relationships as dynamic, interactive AWS Application Composer-style Data Flow Diagrams. This app is architected with DoD Impact Level 5 (IL5) environments, Risk Management Framework (RMF) audits, and Zero-Trust architecture (ZTA) in mind.

search.query: | makeresults count=5 | streamstats count as id | eval from=case(id=1, "Internet", id=2, "arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/app/app-alb/987654321", id=3, "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:order-processor-fn", id=4, "arn:aws:rds:us-east-1:123456789012:db:production-db", id=5, "arn:aws:wafv2:us-east-1:123456789012:regional/webacl/edge-waf/11223344") | eval to=case(id=1, "arn:aws:wafv2:us-east-1:123456789012:regional/webacl/edge-waf/11223344", id=2, "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:order-processor-fn", id=3, "arn:aws:rds:us-east-1:123456789012:db:production-db", id=5, "arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/app/app-alb/987654321") | eval type=case(id=1, "AWS::Resource", id=2, "AWS::Resource", id=3, "AWS::Resource", id=4, "AWS::Resource", id=5, "AWS::Resource") | eval node_label=case(id=1, "Internet", id=2, "App Load Balancer", id=3, "Order Processor Function", id=4, "Production RDS Database", id=5, "App Edge WAF") | eval edge_label=case(id=1, "HTTPS/443", id=2, "HTTPS/8443", id=3, "MySQL/3306", id=5, "Forward") | eval group=case(id=1, "External Zone", id=2, "Security Boundary", id=3, "Compute Boundary", id=4, "Database Boundary", id=5, "Security Boundary") | eval status=case(id=4, "OK", id=3, "OK", id=2, "OK", id=5, "OK", id=1, "OK") | eval icon=case(id=1, "RESOURCE", id=2, "ALB", id=3, "LAMBDA", id=4, "RDS", id=5, "WAF") | table from to type node_label edge_label group status icon

| makeresults | eval from="ROOT_NODE", to="policy-plane-root", type="AWS::IAM::Role", node_label="Zero-Trust Boundary", edge_label="" | append [ | makeresults | eval from="ROOT_NODE", to="data-plane-root", type="AWS::IAM::Role", node_label="Zero-Trust Boundary", edge_label="" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="ROOT_NODE", to="identity-plane-root", type="AWS::IAM::Role", node_label="Zero-Trust Boundary", edge_label="" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="policy-plane-root", to="pe-engine", type="AWS::Resource", node_label="Policy Engine (PE)", edge_label="", group="Policy Plane" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="pe-engine", to="pa-admin", type="AWS::Resource", node_label="Policy Administrator (PA)", edge_label="Decision Channel", group="Policy Plane" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="identity-plane-root", to="developer-user", type="AWS::IAM::User", node_label="Developer User", edge_label="", group="Identity Plane" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="developer-user", to="bastion-host", type="AWS::EC2::Instance", node_label="Bastion Host", edge_label="Assume Role", group="Identity Plane" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="data-plane-root", to="bastion-host", type="AWS::EC2::Instance", node_label="Bastion Host", edge_label="", group="Data Plane" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="bastion-host", to="web-server", type="AWS::EC2::Instance", node_label="Web Server", edge_label="SSH/22", group="Data Plane" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="web-server", to="db-server", type="AWS::RDS::DBInstance", node_label="DB Server", edge_label="SQL/3306", group="Data Plane" ] | fields from to type node_label edge_label group status

div.p: The visualizer reads standard tabular results from your SPL query. You should format your query using | table or | project to yield the following columns:

table.style: color: #dcdcdc; width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse; border: 1px solid #444; margin-top: 10px; margin-bottom: 20px;

td.style: padding: 10px; border: 1px solid #444; font-weight: bold; color: #FF9900;

tr.td: The source node resourceId or full AWS ARN. ARNs are normalized internally for safe D3 selections.

td.style: padding: 10px; border: 1px solid #444; font-weight: bold; color: #879196;

tr.td: The destination node resourceId or ARN. If left empty, an isolated node is drawn without connections.
AWS resource type, used to resolve service-specific architectural icons automatically.
Human-readable label shown inside the node card. Falls back to extracting the short name from the ARN.
Relationship descriptor text overlaying the link curve, enclosed in a clean background halo.
Pillar/security zone name. Nodes of the same group are drawn inside curved, shaded convex hulls.
Configuration status. Statuses like ResourceDeleted render cards with reduced opacity and dashed borders.
Configuration capture timestamp. Older snapshots trigger stale-node fade-out animations.

panel.title: 1.2. Integrated Data Source Targeting & SPL Best Practices

div.p: When query-generating data from live AWS Config JSON tables or building mock datasets, adhere strictly to the following ingestion best practices:

ul.li: JSON Targeting: Base queries on sourcetype="aws:config" or equivalent JSON formats. Utilize spath to extract fields cleanly.
Memory-Safe Relationship Extraction: Always append a limit when expanding relationship arrays (e.g. | mvexpand rels limit=1000) to prevent high-volume workloads from hitting Splunk memory caps.

Schema Field Retention: Ensure extended attributes (subnetId, vpcId, and securityGroups) are explicitly returned in your terminal | table command, even if not represented as nodes. These fields are mandatory to drive active drilldowns and concentric compliance rings.

Mandatory Group Boundaries: Nesting structures correctly requires formatting the group field inline: | eval group = case(isnotnull(vpcId) AND isnotnull(subnetId), vpcId . "/" . subnetId, isnotnull(vpcId), vpcId, 1=1, "GLOBAL_ROOT").
JSON Escaping inside SPL: When hardcoding mock nodes (e.g. via | makeresults), all quote characters inside the JSON strings (such as securityGroups) MUST be escaped with a backslash: \".

Tabular Schema Padding: If using append combinations, initialize empty attributes (e.g. subnetId="" vpcId="" securityGroups="") on non-infrastructure layers to prevent schema mismatch rendering failures.

panel.title: 2. NIST 800-207 Zero-Trust Architecture (ZTA) Copy-Paste SPL Scenarios

div.p: You can visualize NIST 800-207 Zero-Trust logical diagrams directly inside Splunk. Copy and paste either of the following standard self-contained SPL searches into a search panel or dashboard to see ZTA rendering in action:
div.h3: Scenario A: Core PDP/PEP Data Access Path
div.p: This scenario models the logical path of a User Subject requesting access to an Enterprise Resource, intercepted by a Policy Enforcement Point (PEP), which verifies identity and posture policies with the Policy Decision Point (PE / PA):

pre.style: background-color: #1a222a; color: #5cb85c; padding: 12px; border-radius: 4px; border: 1px solid #333; overflow-x: auto; font-family: monospace; font-size: 12px; line-height: 1.4;

div.h3: Scenario B: Multi-Source PIP Query Engine
div.p: This scenario models the Policy Engine (PE) querying multiple distinct Policy Information Points (PIPs)—such as the User Attribute Registry (IAM), MDM Posture Manager (DEVICE), and Activity Logs audit database (CLOUDTRAIL)—to feed the trust algorithm during an evaluation:

pre.style: background-color: #1a222a; color: #5cb85c; padding: 12px; border-radius: 4px; border: 1px solid #333; overflow-x: auto; font-family: monospace; font-size: 12px; line-height: 1.4;

div.h3: Scenario C: Zero-Trust Static Layout & Multi-Plane Compliance Interception
div.p: This scenario models elements across all three Zero-Trust planes (Identity, Policy & Control, and Infrastructure). It features assumed roles, WAF-protected CDNs routing to VPC subnets, compliant/non-compliant security groups, and mid-flight compliance violations (SSH/22 rendered in dashed red):

pre.style: background-color: #1a222a; color: #5cb85c; padding: 12px; border-radius: 4px; border: 1px solid #333; overflow-x: auto; font-family: monospace; font-size: 12px; line-height: 1.4;

div.pre: | makeresults | eval from="user-dev", to="audit-role", type="AWS::IAM::User", node_label="Developer User", edge_label="Assumes Role", subnetId="", vpcId="", securityGroups="" | append [ | makeresults | eval from="audit-role", to="", type="AWS::IAM::Role", node_label="Audit Administrator Role", edge_label="", subnetId="", vpcId="", securityGroups="" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="edge-waf", to="cf-cdn", type="AWS::WAFV2::WebACL", node_label="Edge WAF WebACL", edge_label="Protects CDN", subnetId="", vpcId="", securityGroups="" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="cf-cdn", to="bastion-host", type="AWS::CloudFront::Distribution", node_label="CloudFront CDN", edge_label="Routes Traffic", subnetId="", vpcId="", securityGroups="" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="bastion-host", to="web-server", type="AWS::EC2::Instance", node_label="Bastion Host", edge_label="HTTPS/443", subnetId="subnet-2", vpcId="vpc-1", securityGroups="[{\"id\":\"sg-3\",\"name\":\"bastion-sg\",\"is_compliant\":true}]" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="web-server", to="db-server", type="AWS::EC2::Instance", node_label="Web Server", edge_label="SSH/22", subnetId="subnet-1", vpcId="vpc-1", securityGroups="[{\"id\":\"sg-1\",\"name\":\"web-sg\",\"is_compliant\":true}]" ] | append [ | makeresults | eval from="db-server", to="", type="AWS::RDS::DBInstance", node_label="DB Server", edge_label="", subnetId="subnet-1", vpcId="vpc-1", securityGroups="[{\"id\":\"sg-2\",\"name\":\"db-sg\",\"is_compliant\":false}]" ] | fields from to type node_label edge_label subnetId vpcId securityGroups
div.h3: Scenario D: Live AWS Config Ingestion
div.p: This query handles standard live-data ingestion from `sourcetype="aws:config:json"`. It extracts core resource attributes, formats them to the diagram schema, and uses `mvzip` and `mvexpand` to safely map parent-child relationships and association names without positional shifts:

pre.style: background-color: #1a222a; color: #5cb85c; padding: 12px; border-radius: 4px; border: 1px solid #333; overflow-x: auto; font-family: monospace; font-size: 12px; line-height: 1.4;

div.pre: index=aws sourcetype="aws:config:json" | eval from='configurationItem.resourceId' | eval type='configurationItem.resourceType' | eval node_label=coalesce('configurationItem.resourceName', 'configurationItem.resourceId') | eval group=coalesce('configurationItem.awsRegion', "Global") | eval status='configurationItem.configurationItemStatus' | eval captureTime='configurationItem.configurationItemCaptureTime' | rename configurationItem.relationships{}.resourceId as related_id, configurationItem.relationships{}.relationshipName as relation_name | eval zipped_relations=mvzip(related_id, relation_name, "::") | mvexpand zipped_relations | eval parts=split(zipped_relations, "::") | eval to=mvindex(parts, 0) | eval edge_label=mvindex(parts, 1) | table from to type node_label edge_label group status captureTime
div.p: To establish a stable, aggregated, and compliant static dashboard layout that represents Zero-Trust architecture boundaries, combine the following three mandatory architectural rules:

ul.li: Rule 1: The ROOT_NODE Mathematical Constraint: Hierarchical layouts (non-physics trees) require a single parentless root node to stabilize. The input dataset must inject a virtual parentless node (e.g. ROOT_NODE) for all plane/zone roots to branch from.

Rule 2: The Aggregation & Live-Data Pattern: Instead of listing static templates, use an | inputlookup combined with a | stats count by role | xyseries pattern to dynamically aggregate asset counts into the node labels (e.g., "Policy Engine (4 Active Devices)").

Rule 3: Mandatory Dashboard XML Options: Bypassing Splunk dashboard rendering defects requires explicitly setting and locking the following XML attributes in the dashboard panel configuration:
layoutMode="Hierarchy" - Locks nodes onto static grid positions.
clusterBy="group" - Tells D3 to construct clean, padded rectangular swimlanes wrapping each group.
draggableNodes="false" - Freezes coordinates and disables visual dragging for static briefings.

div.p: Traditional force-graphs frequently suffer from DOM-sync crashes because D3 directly mutates elements managed by React. This visualizer utilizes D3 strictly as a math and physics co-processor to calculate 2D spatial coordinates (handling d3.forceSimulation, collision detection, and zoom matrices), while React retains complete ownership of the DOM (rendering high-fidelity SVG <rect> card elements, images, text, and binding secure events).

When grouping by VPC subnets or security domains (using the group column), the visualizer uses d3.polygonHull combined with a Catmull-Rom closed interpolator to draw shaded convex hulls enclosing the nodes. This allows for immediate visual identification of missing firewalls, insecure trust boundaries, and network segregation breaches in Impact Level 5 (IL5) environments.

In AWS Config relationships, associations are often declared on both ends (e.g. from EC2 pointing to Subnet and vice-versa). The visualizer uses a sorted canonical edge set key ([from, to].sort().join('|')) to automatically prune duplicate connections, preventing D3 forces from double-pulling nodes and collapsing the diagram into visual anomalies.

Traditional D3 force-directed visualizers place a heavy maintenance burden on administrators, who must manually drag cards to organize the layout and save their coordinates. The AWS-DFD-Visualizer completely eliminates this layout persistence overhead via its Pure Deterministic Static Layout Engine. In Blueprint Mode (activated by setting layoutMode="Hierarchy", clusterBy="group", and draggableNodes="false"), new subnets or EC2 scaling groups automatically compute their own structural grid dimensions bottom-up on search refresh. No human dragging, coordinate databases, or manual drag-and-drop maintenance is required.
Customize the presentation of your diagram using the visualizer Format menu in the Edit view:

ul.li: Node Placement Mode (display_mode): Switch between Auto (Physics) to allow forces to self-arrange, or Manual (Sticky) to lock nodes where you drop them.
Layout Engine (layoutMode): Choose Force Directed for organic physics layouts or Hierarchy (Tree) to organize nodes as Top-to-Bottom or Left-to-Right BFS trees.
Clustering (clusterBy): Render security envelopes by group by selecting Group.
Draggability and Zoom (draggableNodes / canZoom): Toggles interactivity for static audit exports.
Edge Styling (smoothEdges): Toggle curved SVG paths versus straight vector lines to maximize link legibility.
